versionNotes: Sprint 4 W13 Look-back 1 (5 Whys) v2 — Step 1 converted to a required reflection gate, 2 questions marked optional, trimmed prompts, closing sentence framed as defense material
title: Look-back 1: 5 Whys
description: 10 points. ~25 minutes. You leave with one honest sentence about how you actually handle assignments — the kind of sentence that makes a defense stand out.

questions.infoBlock:
Step 1 of 6 · Commitment gate

Open your Sprint 1 5 Whys assignment in a new tab and read what you wrote. Don’t try to fix it. Just read it. Then answer the short question below.

🔗 Open Sprint 1: 5 Whys Analysis →
questions.prompt: What surprised you re-reading your original 5 Whys?
questions.placeholder: One or two sentences — just the surprise, not the full analysis yet.

questions.sectionTitle: Step 2: What was the assignment trying to develop in you?
questions.infoBlock:
Step 2 of 6 · The hidden intent

Name the actual capability the assignment was trying to build.

Not what the prompt literally said — what was the actual capability the assignment was trying to build? Name it in your own words.
questions.prompt: In your own words, what was this assignment actually trying to develop in you?
questions.placeholder: Example: It wasn't really about getting to "the" root cause. It was about whether I'd keep asking when the answer started getting uncomfortable.
questions.sparringRole: Help the student distinguish the surface task (do 5 whys) from the deeper capability being built (tolerance for discomfort, honest self-inquiry, willingness to keep asking). If they name the surface task, push gently for what's underneath.

questions.sectionTitle: Step 3: What would a genuine attempt have looked like for you?
questions.infoBlock:
Step 3 of 6 · A genuine attempt · Optional

What would you have had to do, think, or face for this assignment to actually work on you?

Be specific to your situation, not generic. Skip this step if Step 2 already captured what matters.
questions.prompt: What would a genuine attempt at this assignment have looked like for you, specifically?
questions.placeholder: Example: I would've had to admit my "root cause" was really about my dad's expectations, not my study habits — and I wasn't ready to write that down where a professor could read it.
questions.sparringRole: Push the student toward their specific circumstances — generic answers don't help. Ask what was actually in the room for them that week: what they were avoiding, what they were performing, what they were hiding from themselves.

questions.sectionTitle: Step 4: The wrong way — and whether you took it
questions.infoBlock:
Step 4 of 6 · The wrong way · Optional

Name the wrong way to approach this assignment — and say honestly whether you took it.

Step 3 and Step 4 are two angles on the same question. Answer one, both, or neither — whichever sharpens your thinking.
questions.prompt: What would the wrong way to approach this assignment have been — and did you do it that way?
questions.placeholder: Example: The wrong way was to write five whys that each sounded reasonable but stayed above the waterline. I did it that way. I can tell because my final "why" is basically a study-skills tip, not a truth about me.
questions.sparringRole: If the student claims they did it right, ask how they can tell. If they admit they took a shortcut, affirm the honesty and ask what they'd recognize as the signal to not do it again.

questions.infoBlock:
Step 5 of 6 · What you gained

What did you walk away with — or what would you have walked away with if you'd engaged genuinely?
questions.prompt: What did you actually gain from this assignment — or what would you have gained if you'd engaged genuinely?
questions.placeholder: Example: If I'd actually pushed through I'd have a usable warning signal — when my "why" starts sounding reasonable too fast, that's exactly the moment I'm lying to myself. That would've been worth the discomfort.
questions.sparringRole: If the student names a concrete gain, affirm it and ask how they'd recognize it in their next assignment. If they describe a missed gain hypothetically, push for what it would have felt like in their body — abstractions get them less than felt recognition.

questions.infoBlock:
Step 6 of 6 · Closing sentence · No AI

One or two sentences. What does this look-back reveal about how you handle hard truths about yourself?

No AI help on this one — it's yours.

The clearest thing you'll say about yourself in your defense likely starts here. Save this sentence.
questions.prompt: In one or two sentences: what does this look-back reveal about how you handle hard truths about yourself?
questions.placeholder: One or two sentences. Yours alone.
